wiki_catalogs.name: Project Overview
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive content for the Git Magager project overview. Explain the project's purpose as a Chrome extension that simplifies Git repository cloning from GitHub and GitLab without requiring terminal commands. Document the hybrid architecture combining Chrome extension components with a native host server. Describe the key benefits including one-click cloning, automatic URL detection, floating clone buttons, and terminal automation. Include system requirements, compatibility with different Git hosting platforms, and the overall user workflow. Provide context for both beginners new to Git operations and experienced developers looking for efficient repository management tools.

wiki_catalogs.name: Manifest Configuration
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for Git Magager's Chrome extension manifest configuration. Detail the manifest v3 structure, permission requirements (storage, activeTab, scripting), host_permissions for GitHub and GitLab domains, and security model. Explain the service worker registration, action button configuration with popup and icons, content script injection settings, and options page setup. Document the run_at timing for content scripts, CSP implications, and cross-origin resource sharing considerations. Include best practices for manifest security, permission minimization, and Chrome Web Store submission requirements.

wiki_catalogs.name: Popup Interface
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for the Git Magager popup interface component. Detail the popup window layout including the header with logo and title, server status indicator with visual feedback, and main clone interface section. Document the URL input field with placeholder text and validation, HTTPS/SSH protocol radio buttons, terminal toggle switch, and clone button functionality. Explain the server connectivity status display with color-coded indicators and loading states. Cover the error handling for disconnected servers with clear instructions for users. Include the navigation to settings page and version display. Provide CSS styling details and responsive design considerations.

wiki_catalogs.name: URL Detection and Parsing
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for Git Magager's URL detection and parsing functionality. Detail the platform detection logic for GitHub and GitLab repositories, including hostname matching and domain pattern recognition. Document the multiple URL extraction methods used for each platform, including direct URL parsing from page elements, attribute-based detection, and fallback mechanisms. Explain the GitHub-specific URL extraction techniques using query selectors targeting clone buttons, input fields, and clipboard attributes. Cover GitLab URL detection including namespace handling, path cleaning, and SSH vs HTTPS protocol differentiation. Include error handling for edge cases, unsupported pages, and malformed URLs. Provide examples of detected URL formats and explain the precedence order for different detection methods.

wiki_catalogs.name: HTTP Server Implementation
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the HTTP server implementation. Document the server initialization, port configuration (localhost:9456), and CORS policy setup. Explain the request routing logic for different endpoints (/health, /config, /clone). Detail the request/response handling patterns, including GET and POST method processing. Cover error handling strategies, status code responses, and JSON serialization. Include examples of client-server communication patterns and debugging techniques for local HTTP communication.

wiki_catalogs.name: HTTP Endpoints
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive HTTP API documentation for Git Magager's local server endpoints. Document the /health endpoint for server status verification, including response format and health indicators. Detail the /config endpoint for configuration management with GET requests to retrieve current settings and POST requests to update configuration values. Document the /clone endpoint for repository cloning operations, including request parameters (URL, openTerminal flag), response formats, and error handling. Specify HTTP methods, URL patterns, content-type requirements, and CORS configuration. Include request/response schemas, parameter validation rules, and error response formats. Provide practical examples of API usage with curl commands and client implementation patterns. Address authentication considerations, rate limiting implications, and security headers.

wiki_catalogs.name: Getting Started
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive getting started documentation for Git Magager. Include step-by-step installation instructions for the Chrome extension, native host server setup, and initial configuration. Document system requirements including Chrome browser compatibility, Node.js installation, and Git CLI prerequisites. Provide clear setup instructions for the native messaging host, launchd service installation on macOS, and verification steps. Include basic usage examples showing how to clone repositories from GitHub and GitLab using both the popup interface and floating clone buttons. Address common installation issues and troubleshooting steps for first-time users.

wiki_catalogs.name: Background Service Worker
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed content for the background service worker architecture. Explain the service worker lifecycle, initialization sequence, and long-running process management. Document server communication protocols, health check mechanisms, and error handling strategies. Detail message passing patterns with popup and content scripts, event listener management, and cross-origin communication security. Include service worker update mechanisms, debugging techniques, and performance optimization strategies. Address Chrome extension security boundaries and sandbox limitations.

wiki_catalogs.name: Options and Settings Page
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed documentation for the Git Magager options and settings management interface. Document the settings page layout and form controls for configuring clone directory path, terminal application selection (Terminal/iTerm2/Warp), and preference persistence. Explain the configuration validation, default value handling, and settings synchronization with the native host server. Detail the user interface elements including file picker dialogs, dropdown menus, and form submission handling. Cover the settings backup and restore functionality, if implemented. Include accessibility features and keyboard navigation support for the settings interface.

wiki_catalogs.name: Clone Button Injection
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop detailed documentation for the clone button injection system. Explain the floating clone button creation process, including DOM element construction, CSS class assignment, and positioning strategies. Document the dual-button architecture with main clone button and protocol selection dropdown for HTTPS/SSH options. Detail the GitHub-specific page integration button that appears alongside existing repository actions. Cover the injection timing mechanisms including initial page load, SPA navigation handling via MutationObserver, and URL change detection. Explain the prevention of duplicate injections, element cleanup, and dynamic removal when users navigate away from repository pages. Include CSS styling details, SVG icon implementation, and responsive design considerations. Document the event handling system for button clicks, dropdown interactions, and external click detection for dropdown closure.

wiki_catalogs.name: Git Operation Handlers
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop comprehensive documentation for Git operation handlers. Detail the cloneRepo function implementation including command construction, process execution using child_process.exec, and output capture. Document the clone directory management, ensuring directory existence and recursive creation. Explain the promise-based async handling, error propagation, and success/failure response formatting. Include examples of supported Git protocols, URL handling, and repository cloning workflows. Address common Git errors and their resolution strategies.

wiki_catalogs.name: Extension Messaging
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for Chrome extension messaging interfaces used in Git Magager. Document the message passing architecture between extension components including background script, popup, options page, and content scripts. Specify message types, payload structures, and response handling patterns. Detail the communication flow between extension components and the native host server via HTTP requests. Document message validation, error propagation, and timeout handling. Include practical examples of message sending and receiving, asynchronous operation handling, and state synchronization between components. Address message serialization, data transformation, and cross-origin communication considerations. Provide debugging techniques for message flow issues and integration patterns with the native messaging protocol.

wiki_catalogs.name: User Interface Guide
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed user interface documentation for Git Magager's Chrome extension components. Document the popup interface for direct cloning operations, including URL input validation, protocol selection (HTTPS/SSH), and clone button functionality. Explain the options page interface for configuration management, including clone directory settings, terminal application selection, and preference persistence. Detail the floating clone buttons injected into GitHub and GitLab repository pages, including positioning, styling, and interaction patterns. Provide visual descriptions of UI states, error messages, loading indicators, and user feedback mechanisms. Include accessibility considerations and keyboard navigation support.

wiki_catalogs.name: Content Script Integration
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for content script architecture and DOM integration. Explain URL pattern matching for GitHub and GitLab repositories, DOM manipulation techniques, and floating clone button injection. Document the content script lifecycle, execution context, and communication with background scripts. Detail CSS styling approaches, responsive design considerations, and cross-browser compatibility. Include security restrictions, CSP implications, and best practices for content script development. Address performance optimization, memory management, and debugging content scripts.

wiki_catalogs.name: Floating Clone Buttons
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the floating clone buttons injected into GitHub and GitLab repository pages. Document the button positioning strategy, visual styling with custom CSS, and integration with the target website's design system. Explain the button injection mechanism, DOM manipulation techniques, and dynamic positioning based on page layout. Detail the button states including hover effects, click handlers, and loading indicators during clone operations. Cover the responsive design considerations for different screen sizes and page layouts. Include the CSS styling approach and customization options for button appearance.

wiki_catalogs.name: Git Operation Execution
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the Git operation execution pipeline. Explain the HTTP communication flow between the content script and native host server, including POST request formatting and response handling. Document the clone operation implementation, including URL parameter passing, server-side Git command execution, and progress reporting. Cover the user interface feedback system during cloning operations, including loading states, success indicators, and error display mechanisms. Detail the notification system for user feedback, including success messages, error notifications, and timeout handling. Explain the button state management during operations, including disabling mechanisms and automatic restoration. Include error handling strategies for network failures, server unavailability, and Git operation errors. Document the integration with the native host server and the local HTTP communication protocol.

wiki_catalogs.name: Configuration Management
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create detailed documentation for the configuration management system. Document the configuration file structure (.git-magager.json in user home directory), default configuration values, and JSON persistence mechanisms. Explain the loadConfig and saveConfig functions including file I/O operations, error handling, and configuration merging strategies. Detail the configuration schema with cloneDirectory, openInTerminal, and terminalApp properties. Include examples of configuration updates, validation rules, and migration strategies for configuration changes.

wiki_catalogs.name: Core Functionality
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for Git Magager's core functionality. Detail the one-click cloning workflow from URL detection to repository cloning, including automatic URL parsing for GitHub and GitLab repositories. Document the Git operation execution process, including HTTPS and SSH protocol support, authentication handling, and progress reporting. Explain the floating clone button injection mechanism, including DOM manipulation, event handling, and integration with target websites. Cover the terminal automation feature, including supported terminal applications (Terminal, iTerm2, Warp), AppleScript implementation, and command preparation. Include error handling strategies, timeout management, and user notification systems.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop comprehensive documentation for Chrome extension message passing architecture. Explain asynchronous message processing, error propagation, and timeout management. Include security considerations for message validation, origin checking, and data sanitization. Address debugging message passing issues, performance optimization, and best practices for structured communication in Chrome extensions.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Develop comprehensive documentation for the terminal automation feature. Document the security considerations and user consent mechanisms for automated terminal operations.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Document the terminal automation system for macOS applications. Detail the openInTerminal function implementation including AppleScript integration for Terminal, iTerm, and Warp applications. Explain the command construction for different terminal applications, script execution, and process management. Cover the terminal command preparation including directory navigation and Git clone execution. Document the promise-based async handling, error propagation, and platform-specific considerations. Include examples of terminal automation workflows and troubleshooting common automation issues.

wiki_catalogs.name: Extension Architecture
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create architectural documentation for Git Magager's Chrome extension components. Describe the high-level design including background service worker architecture, content script injection patterns, and message passing systems. Document the manifest v3 configuration, permission requirements, and security model. Explain component interactions between background scripts, content scripts, and popup interfaces. Detail the service worker lifecycle management, server communication protocols, and cross-origin resource sharing considerations. Include data flow diagrams showing information exchange between extension components and the native host server. Address security considerations, sandboxing limitations, and best practices for Chrome extension development.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for launchd service integration and auto-start functionality. wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive documentation for the native host server component. Detail the HTTP server implementation including endpoint definitions (/health, /config, /clone), request/response handling, and error management. Document the Git operation handlers for cloning repositories, including command execution, process management, and output capture. Explain the configuration management system, including JSON file persistence, default value handling, and settings validation. Cover the launchd service integration for automatic startup, service registration, and system integration. Include security considerations, local server communication protocols, and debugging techniques. Address platform-specific implementations and cross-platform compatibility challenges.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive API documentation for Git Magager's HTTP endpoints and extension messaging interfaces. Document all REST API endpoints including /health for server status checks, /config for configuration management, and /clone for repository cloning operations. For each endpoint, specify HTTP methods, URL patterns, request/response schemas, parameter validation rules, and error response formats. Document the Chrome extension messaging system including message types, payload structures, and response handling. Include authentication methods, rate limiting considerations, and security headers. Provide practical examples of API usage, client implementation guidelines, and integration patterns. Address error codes, exception handling, and debugging approaches for API consumers.

wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive development documentation for Git Magager contributors. Document the codebase structure, development environment setup, and build processes. wiki_catalogs.name: Troubleshooting Guide
wiki_catalogs.prompt: Create comprehensive troubleshooting documentation for Git Magager. wiki_overview.content:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the project. Let me create the analysis:

<blog>

# Git Magager: Chrome Extension with Native Host for One-Click Git Cloning

## Project Introduction

**Git Magager** is a Chrome extension designed to simplify Git repository cloning operations by providing a seamless one-click experience for developers. The project consists of two main components: a Chrome extension that provides the user interface and browser integration, and a native host server that handles the actual Git operations locally on the user's machine.

### Purpose Statement
The primary goal is to eliminate the need for terminal commands when cloning repositories from popular platforms like GitHub and GitLab, making Git operations more accessible to developers who prefer GUI-based workflows.

### Core Goals and Objectives
- **Simplify Git Operations**: Provide intuitive one-click cloning without terminal knowledge
- **Multi-Platform Support**: Support both GitHub and GitLab repositories
- **Flexible Configuration**: Allow users to customize clone locations and terminal preferences
- **Cross-Platform Compatibility**: Work seamlessly across different development environments

### Target Audience
- Developers who want to avoid terminal commands for Git operations
- Teams seeking standardized repository cloning procedures
- Users transitioning from GUI-based development tools to Git

## Technical Architecture

The project follows a client-server architecture pattern with clear separation of concerns between the browser extension and the native host server.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Chrome Extension] --> B[Background Service Worker]
    A --> C[Content Scripts]
    A --> D[Popup Interface]
    A --> E[Options Page]
    
    B --> F[Native Host Server]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> G[Local File System]
    F --> H[Git CLI]
    F --> I[Terminal Applications]
    
    J[GitHub/GitLab Pages] --> C
    K[User Input] --> D
```

### Component Breakdown

**Chrome Extension Components:**
- **Background Service Worker**: Handles messaging between extension components and manages server communication
- **Content Scripts**: Injects clone buttons into GitHub and GitLab pages
- **Popup Interface**: Provides direct cloning functionality from the extension icon
- **Options Page**: Allows configuration of clone settings and preferences

**Native Host Server:**
- **HTTP Server**: Provides RESTful endpoints for extension communication
- **Git Operations**: Executes actual clone commands and manages repository downloads
- **Configuration Management**: Handles user preferences and settings persistence

### Design Patterns

The project implements several key design patterns:

1. **Service Worker Pattern**: Background service worker manages long-running processes
2. **Content Script Injection**: Dynamic injection of UI elements into target websites
3. **Message Passing**: Structured communication between extension components
4. **Configuration Pattern**: Persistent settings management with default fallbacks

### System Relationships

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Extension
    participant Background
    participant Server
    participant Git
    
    User->>Extension: Click Clone Button
    Extension->>Background: Send Message
    Background->>Server: HTTP Request
    Server->>Git: Execute Clone Command
    Git-->>Server: Clone Result
    Server-->>Background: Response
    Background-->>Extension: Status Update
    Extension-->>User: Visual Feedback
```

## Key Implementation

### Main Entry Points

**Chrome Extension Entry Point:**
- [`chrome-extension/manifest.json`](chrome-extension/manifest.json) - Defines extension permissions, background script, and content scripts

**Native Host Entry Point:**
- [`native-host/server.js`](native-host/server.js) - HTTP server handling all Git operations

### Core Modules

**Background Service Worker:**
- [`chrome-extension/background.js`](chrome-extension/background.js) - Manages extension lifecycle and server communication

**Content Script Implementation:**
- [`chrome-extension/content.js`](chrome-extension/content.js) - Handles URL detection, button injection, and clone execution

**Popup Interface:**
- [`chrome-extension/popup.js`](chrome-extension/popup.js) - Provides direct cloning functionality from extension icon

**Options Management:**
- [`chrome-extension/options.js`](chrome-extension/options.js) - Handles user configuration settings

### Configuration Approach

**Extension Configuration:**
- [`chrome-extension/manifest.json`](chrome-extension/manifest.json) - Defines permissions, host permissions, and resource declarations

**Native Host Configuration:**
- [`native-host/server.js`](native-host/server.js) - Implements JSON-based configuration with default values
- [`native-host/setup.sh`](native-host/setup.sh) - Auto-start service installation using launchd

### External Dependencies

**Chrome Extension Dependencies:**
- Standard Chrome Extension APIs (runtime, tabs, storage)
- No external npm dependencies required

**Native Host Dependencies:**
- [`native-host/package.json`](native-host/package.json) - Minimal dependencies with Node.js built-ins only

### Integration Points

**Browser Integration:**
- GitHub and GitLab URL pattern matching
- DOM manipulation for UI injection
- Clipboard API integration for URL extraction

**System Integration:**
- Local HTTP server communication
- Terminal application automation
- File system operations for repository storage

### Component Relationship Diagrams

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Manifest] --> B[Background]
    A --> C[Content Scripts]
    A --> D[Popup]
    A --> E[Options]
    
    B --> F[Server Communication]
    C --> G[UI Injection]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    
    F --> H[Local Server]
    H --> I[Git Operations]
    H --> J[File System]
```

## Key Features

### Functionality Overview

**One-Click Cloning:**
- Automatic detection of repository URLs on GitHub and GitLab
- Floating clone buttons injected into repository pages
- Support for both HTTPS and SSH clone protocols

**Multi-Terminal Support:**
- Integration with macOS Terminal, iTerm2, and Warp
- Automatic terminal launching with prepared commands
- Customizable terminal application selection

**Smart Configuration:**
- Default clone directory management
- Persistent user preferences
- Automatic server health monitoring

### Implementation Highlights

**URL Detection and Parsing:**
- [`chrome-extension/content.js`](chrome-extension/content.js) - Implements sophisticated URL parsing for multiple repository platforms
- Handles edge cases and various page layouts across GitHub and GitLab

**Server Communication Protocol:**
- [`chrome-extension/background.js`](chrome-extension/background.js) - Defines structured messaging between extension components
- [`native-host/server.js`](native-host/server.js) - Implements RESTful API endpoints for extension communication

**User Interface Components:**
- [`chrome-extension/popup.html`](chrome-extension/popup.html) - Clean, modern popup interface with real-time feedback
- [`chrome-extension/options.html`](chrome-extension/options.html) - Dark-themed settings page with comprehensive configuration options

### Feature Architecture Diagrams

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ExtensionInstalled
    ExtensionInstalled --> ServerConnected: Background Checks
    ExtensionInstalled --> ServerDisconnected: Health Check Failed
    
    ServerConnected --> Ready: Config Loaded
    ServerDisconnected --> SetupRequired: First Launch
    
    Ready --> Cloning: User Initiates Clone
    Ready --> Configuring: User Changes Settings
    
    Cloning --> Success: Clone Complete
    Cloning --> Error: Clone Failed
    
    Success --> Ready: Reset State
    Error --> Ready: Show Error Message
    
    Configuring --> Ready: Settings Saved
```

**Advanced Features:**
- [`native-host/server.js`](native-host/server.js) - Implements terminal automation using AppleScript
- [`chrome-extension/content.js`](chrome-extension/content.js) - Handles SPA navigation and dynamic content injection
- [`native-host/setup.sh`](native-host/setup.sh) - Provides automated service installation and management

**Security Considerations:**
- Local-only server communication (127.0.0.1)
- Strict CORS policy implementation
- Permission-based access control

The project demonstrates excellent separation of concerns with the Chrome extension handling user interaction and the native host managing system-level operations, creating a robust and user-friendly Git cloning experience.

**Sources:**
- [manifest.json](chrome-extension/manifest.json)
- [background.js](chrome-extension/background.js)
- [content.js](chrome-extension/content.js)
- [popup.js](chrome-extension/popup.js)
- [options.js](chrome-extension/options.js)
- [server.js](native-host/server.js)
- [package.json](native-host/package.json)
- [setup.sh](native-host/setup.sh)

</blog>
